name: Ama-no-Habakiri
description: Ama-no-Habakiri (天羽々斬) is a legendary sword in Japanese mythology, primarily known for its role in the myth of Susano-o-no-Mikoto slaying the Yamata no Orochi, an eight-headed serpent. The name translates to "Heavenly Feather Cutter," suggesting its exceptional sharpness and ability to slice through anything with ease. According to the Kojiki and Nihon Shoki, Susano-o discovered the sword within the body of the Yamata no Orochi after slaying it. The sword was originally called Totsuka-no-Tsurugi (十拳剣, "Ten-Grip Sword") or Worochi-no-Aramasa. However, after Susano-o used it to cut the serpent into pieces and found that the blade was slightly notched, he renamed it Ama-no-Habakiri. Within the serpent's tail, he discovered the Kusanagi-no-Tsurugi, which he then presented to Amaterasu, his sister, as a peace offering. Ama-no-Habakiri is considered a sacred blade, embodying power, protection, and divine justice. Its association with Susano-o's heroic act cemented its place in Japanese folklore as a symbol of overcoming great evil and establishing order. The sword's legacy continues to be celebrated in various artistic and cultural expressions, representing the triumph of good over evil and the importance of courage and strength. While the Kusanagi-no-Tsurugi is more widely known today, Ama-no-Habakiri holds a significant place in the sword's origin story and Susano-o's transformation from a mischievous god to a heroic figure.
subtitle: Heavenly Feather Cutter, Serpent Slayer
origin: Forged by the gods or discovered by Susano-o

keyMyths.title: Susano-o and the Yamata no Orochi
keyMyths.description: Susano-o slays the eight-headed serpent, Yamata no Orochi, using the Ama-no-Habakiri. Within the serpent's tail, he discovers the Kusanagi-no-Tsurugi.

keyMyths.title: Naming of the Sword
keyMyths.description: The sword was initially called Totsuka-no-Tsurugi, but after it was notched during the slaying of the serpent, Susano-o renamed it Ama-no-Habakiri.

sources.author: Donald L. Philippi
sources.description: Translation of the Kojiki, a primary source for Japanese mythology.
sources.text: Translation of the Kojiki, a primary source for Japanese mythology.
